top_categories.workout_context: 13,543ft of climbing means repeated surges above threshold. VO2max work is how you survive the fifth climb, not just the first.
At mile 60 of 107, glycogen is gone. This workout teaches your body to produce watts on fumes.
Race-pace efforts that mimic Granfondo La Fausto Coppi's demands. Pacing, fueling, and tactical decisions under fatigue. Practice the race before June.
Every hard session works better with a bigger aerobic base. At 107 miles, base fitness is the difference between racing and surviving.
127ft/mi means long efforts at or near FTP on every climb. If your threshold cracks at minute 15, you'll walk the rest.
88–92% FTP is where you'll spend the hardest sustained miles of Granfondo La Fausto Coppi. Train it until it feels boring.
Granfondo La Fausto Coppi's paved Italian mountain roads through the Maritime forces constant power changes. Surge over the rough stuff, settle on the smooth, repeat for 107 miles.
Endurance with intensity spikes baked in. Exactly what 13,543ft of climbing on paved Italian mountain roads through the Maritime does to your power file on race day.
107 miles rewards fat oxidation. This volume work shifts your fuel mix so you're still burning fat at hour 5, not bonking at hour 3.
At Granfondo La Fausto Coppi, surges push you above threshold and you have to recover while still riding. Over-unders train exactly that.
Short, max efforts are unavoidable. Punchy climbs, gap closures, attacks on paved Italian mountain roads through the Maritime. If you haven't trained them, you'll crack.
Two threshold sessions per week without the recovery cost of VO2max work. For 107 miles, sustainable FTP gains matter more than peak power.

race_overlay.nutrition: Target 60–80g carbs/hour for Granfondo La Fausto Coppi’s 107 miles. Start fueling within the first 30 minutes — early fueling prevents late-race collapse. Bonking at mile 60 is a nutrition failure, not a fitness failure.
race_overlay.altitude: Granfondo La Fausto Coppi with 13,543ft of climbing, much of it above 8,000ft demands altitude preparation. Arrive 5–7 days early for acclimatization. Expect 5–15% power reduction at altitude. Increase iron intake 4 weeks out. Hydrate aggressively — altitude increases fluid loss by 20–40%. Summit temperatures at Colle Fauniera (2,481m) can drop to 8-12C even in late June — a 15-18C differential from the valley start.

pack_summary: This 10-workout pack focuses on VO2max, Durability, and Race Simulation to prepare you for 107 miles of paved italian mountain roads through the maritime alps and piedmont foothills in Cuneo, Piedmont, Italy.
